attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of an intensely itchy rash on her forearms and legs. The rash developed after spending a day at her Aunt's hobby farm where she was weeding the carrot and celery plots. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She has a new boyfriend and states that they are sexually active and uses condoms. She reports using fragranced moisturiser daily without problems.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is an erythematous, eczematous eruption with small vesicles affecting sun-exposed areas of her forearms, with sharp demarcation at the sleeve lines, and her lower legs bilaterally. The rash spares the area under her watch strap. The skin on her torso is unaffected.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a classic case of photocontact dermatitis, characterised by an eczematous reaction occurring in sun-exposed areas following contact with a photosensitising agent (in this case, likely secondary to contact with furocoumarins from carrots and celery). The key diagnostic feature is the distribution pattern - affecting only sun-exposed areas with sparing of areas covered by clothing and accessories. This type IV hypersensitivity reaction due to the presence of ultraviolet light can cause sunburn-like appearance to porphyria-like reactions.

Photocontact dermatitis requires both a photosensitising agent and UV exposure to produce symptoms. The sharp demarcation at clothing lines and sparing under the watch strap are particularly characteristic features. Common causes include sunscreens containing benzophenones, fragrances, and certain medications such as tetracyclines, sulfonamides, amiodarone and NSAIDS.

Secondary syphilis typically presents with a generalised, symmetrical, non-itchy, copper-coloured maculopapular rash affecting the trunk, palms and soles. It would not show photodistribution or sparing under clothing.

Polymorphic light eruption is a primary photosensitivity disorder that occurs without requiring a photosensitising agent. It typically recurs each spring/summer and improves with continued sun exposure (hardening phenomenon).

Actinic prurigo is a chronic photosensitivity disorder that typically begins in childhood and affects Native Americans more commonly. It presents with intensely pruritic papules and nodules on sun-exposed areas, but unlike this case, is persistent and often associated with facial involvement.

Allergic contact dermatitis would affect all areas where the allergen contacts the skin, regardless of sun exposure. The sparing of covered areas makes this diagnosis less likely.

Management involves identifying and avoiding the photosensitising agent, using appropriate sun protection, and treating the acute reaction with topical corticosteroids if needed. Patch testing with photopatch testing may be helpful in identifying the specific causative agent.
learningPoints: Photocontact dermatitis presents with an eczematous reaction in sun-exposed areas following exposure to a photosensitising agent.
Common photosensitising agents include sunscreens, fragrances, and certain medications.
Distribution of the rash sparing areas covered by clothing is a key diagnostic feature of photocontact dermatitis.
references: Tahoun A, Ngan V, Coulson I. Photocontact dermatitis. DermNet NZ. 2023.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/photocontact-dermatitis

Oiso N, Kawada A. Review of allergic and photoallergic contact dermatitis from an ingredient in a medicament vehicle consisting of a compress, poultice, plaster, and tape. J Allergy (Cairo). 2011.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3095886/
